summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a chemical reaction, and what are the main types that occur in biological systems?

Chemical reactions obey the law of conservation of mass (Lavoisier, 1789) — atoms are rearranged, not created or destroyed. In biology, major reaction types include: (1) Synthesis/anabolic — building macromolecules (dehydration synthesis linking amino acids into proteins). (2) Decomposition/catabolic — breaking molecules down (hydrolysis of ATP → ADP + Pi). (3) Redox — electron transfer (cellular respiration: glucose oxidized, O₂ reduced). (4) Exchange — parts of reactants swap. The human body performs ~37 billion billion chemical reactions per second, virtually all catalyzed by enzymes. Energy changes in reactions are described by Gibbs free energy (ΔG): negative ΔG = spontaneous/exergonic; positive ΔG = non-spontaneous/endergonic.

What is an enzyme, and what makes it different from inorganic catalysts?

Enzymes are remarkable biological catalysts: (1) Speed — carbonic anhydrase catalyzes ~1 million reactions/second (CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃); without the enzyme, this reaction takes ~15 seconds per molecule. (2) Specificity — each enzyme typically catalyzes one reaction with one substrate (or a small group of related substrates). (3) Not consumed — they emerge unchanged and can repeat the catalytic cycle. (4) Regulated — enzyme activity is controlled by allosteric regulation, covalent modification, and gene expression. Almost all enzymes are proteins (named with '-ase' suffix: lactase, ATP synthase, DNA polymerase), but some RNA molecules (ribozymes) also catalyze reactions — notably the ribosome's peptidyl transferase activity. The human genome encodes ~20,000+ enzymes.

What is activation energy, and how do enzymes lower it?

Activation energy (Ea) is the energy barrier between reactants and the transition state (the unstable intermediate). Enzymes lower Ea (sometimes by 10⁴ to 10⁶-fold) through several mechanisms: (1) Orientation — binding substrates in the optimal position for bond formation/breaking. (2) Strain — slightly distorting substrate bonds toward the transition state geometry (induced fit). (3) Microenvironment — providing an active site with optimal charge distribution, hydrophobicity, or pH. (4) Covalent catalysis — forming temporary covalent bonds with the substrate (e.g., serine proteases). (5) Metal ion catalysis — using cofactors to stabilize charged intermediates. Crucially, enzymes do NOT change the ΔG (thermodynamics) — they only change how fast equilibrium is reached (kinetics). A reaction that is thermodynamically unfavorable will not proceed regardless of enzyme presence.

What is the active site of an enzyme, and how do the lock-and-key and induced fit models describe enzyme-substrate interaction?

The active site comprises only ~10-20 of the enzyme's amino acids, arranged in a precise 3D configuration by the protein's tertiary structure. Fischer's lock-and-key model (1894) envisioned the active site as a rigid lock matching only one substrate key — explaining specificity but not dynamics. Koshland's induced fit model (1958) added that both enzyme and substrate adjust shape upon binding — hexokinase, for example, closes around glucose like a hand closing around a ball, excluding water and positioning glucose perfectly for phosphorylation. The active site often contains: (1) binding residues (non-covalent interactions holding substrate), (2) catalytic residues (directly participating in bond-breaking/forming), and (3) cofactor binding sites.

What is denaturation, and why is it significant for enzyme function?

Denaturation unfolds the enzyme's tertiary (and quaternary) structure by disrupting non-covalent bonds (hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, hydrophobic interactions) — and sometimes covalent disulfide bonds. Examples: (1) Cooking an egg — albumin protein denatures irreversibly at ~63°C, turning from clear to white. (2) Fever — at 40°C+, human enzymes begin losing function; at 42°C sustained core temperature is life-threatening. (3) Heavy metals (Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺) — bind to sulfhydryl (-SH) groups, disrupting disulfide bridges. (4) Strong acids/bases — protonate/deprotonate amino acid R-groups. Some denaturation is reversible: chaperone proteins (heat shock proteins/Hsp70, Hsp90) can help refold mildly denatured proteins. Anfinsen's dogma (Nobel 1972) established that the amino acid sequence determines the 3D fold.

What is feedback inhibition, and how does it regulate metabolic pathways?

Feedback inhibition is a cornerstone of metabolic regulation: (1) Classic example — isoleucine biosynthesis: threonine → (5 steps) → isoleucine. When isoleucine accumulates, it allosterically inhibits threonine deaminase (the first enzyme), shutting down the pathway. When isoleucine levels drop, inhibition is relieved and production resumes. (2) ATP regulation — high ATP allosterically inhibits phosphofructokinase (PFK-1), the committed step of glycolysis; when ATP is consumed and AMP accumulates, PFK-1 is activated. (3) Cholesterol synthesis — cholesterol inhibits HMG-CoA reductase (the rate-limiting enzyme). This is also the target of statin drugs. Feedback inhibition is typically reversible and non-covalent (the product binds and unbinds the allosteric site rapidly), providing dynamic, real-time control of metabolic flux.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: SCENARIO: During the 1995 Tokyo subway attack, the nerve agent sarin was released, affecting thousands of people. Sarin is an organophosphate that irreversibly inhibits acetylcholinesterase (AChE), the enzyme responsible for breaking down the neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh) in synaptic clefts. AChE is one of the fastest enzymes known, with a catalytic rate (kcat) of ~25,000 reactions per second. When AChE is inhibited, ACh accumulates at neuromuscular junctions, causing continuous muscle stimulation — leading to muscle twitching, paralysis of respiratory muscles, excessive secretions, and potentially death. Emergency treatment involves atropine (blocks ACh receptors) and pralidoxime (reactivates AChE if administered before the organophosphate 'ages' — an irreversible covalent modification). How does sarin's mechanism differ from reversible enzyme inhibition?

Irreversible inhibition is fundamentally different from reversible inhibition: Sarin phosphorylates the active-site serine (Ser203) in AChE via a covalent bond, permanently blocking the enzyme. New AChE molecules must be synthesized to restore function (takes days to weeks). Other irreversible inhibitors: (1) Aspirin — irreversibly acetylates Ser530 in cyclooxygenase (COX-1/COX-2), blocking prostaglandin/thromboxane synthesis for the platelet's entire lifespan (~7-10 days). (2) Penicillin — irreversibly acylates the active-site serine of bacterial transpeptidase (DD-transpeptidase), preventing cell wall cross-linking. (3) DFMO (difluoromethylornithine) — irreversible ('suicide') inhibitor of ornithine decarboxylase, used to treat African sleeping sickness. Pralidoxime works by nucleophilic attack on the phosphoryl group, liberating the serine — but only before the 'aging' reaction removes an alkyl group, making the bond truly permanent.

Example: SCENARIO: A pharmaceutical company is developing a new drug to lower cholesterol. They design a molecule that mimics the structure of HMG-CoA (the natural substrate of HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis in the liver). The drug candidate binds to the active site of HMG-CoA reductase with 10,000-fold higher affinity than the natural substrate. This is the same mechanism used by the statin drug family — atorvastatin (Lipitor), the best-selling pharmaceutical in history (~$125 billion in lifetime sales), was designed using X-ray crystallography of the enzyme-inhibitor complex. Statins have reduced cardiovascular mortality by 25-35% in clinical trials. Why is competitive inhibition an effective strategy for this drug, and what advantage does the high binding affinity provide?

Rational drug design exploits enzyme inhibition: (1) Statins occupy HMG-CoA reductase's active site, blocking the conversion of HMG-CoA to mevalonate (the committed step in cholesterol synthesis). (2) High binding affinity (Ki << Km) means the drug dominates the active site even at low concentrations, reducing the required dose and side effects. (3) The liver compensates by upregulating LDL receptors (via SREBP pathway), pulling LDL cholesterol from the blood — the primary therapeutic benefit. (4) Modern statins were optimized using structure-based drug design: X-ray crystallography revealed the enzyme's 3D structure, enabling precise molecular fitting. This demonstrates the practical importance of understanding enzyme kinetics — knowing Ki, Km, and binding mechanisms directly informs drug development. Similar competitive inhibition strategies: ACE inhibitors (enalapril, for hypertension), methotrexate (DHFR, for cancer), and HIV protease inhibitors (saquinavir, ritonavir).

Example: SCENARIO: After consuming several alcoholic drinks, a person experiences facial flushing, rapid heartbeat, nausea, and headache. Genetic testing reveals they carry two copies of the ALDH2*2 variant — a dominant-negative mutation (Glu504Lys) in the aldehyde dehydrogenase 2 (ALDH2) gene that reduces enzyme activity by >95%. Alcohol metabolism proceeds in two steps: (1) Alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) converts ethanol to acetaldehyde (a toxic carcinogen), and (2) ALDH2 converts acetaldehyde to harmless acetate. With deficient ALDH2, acetaldehyde accumulates to levels 6-20 times higher than normal. The ALDH2*2 variant is carried by ~540 million people (~8% of world population), predominantly in East Asian populations (30-50% prevalence). Paradoxically, heterozygous carriers have a 6-10 fold increased risk of esophageal cancer if they drink alcohol. What does this case demonstrate about the consequences of reduced enzyme activity in a metabolic pathway?

The ALDH2*2 variant illustrates critical enzyme pathway principles: (1) Sequential dependency — in the pathway ethanol → acetaldehyde → acetate, blocking step 2 causes the toxic intermediate (acetaldehyde) to accumulate while step 1 continues producing it. (2) Dominant-negative mutation — ALDH2 is a tetramer; even one mutant subunit (Glu504Lys) disrupts the entire complex's function, explaining why heterozygotes also experience flushing. (3) Dose-response — acetaldehyde is a Group 1 carcinogen (IARC); its accumulation at 6-20× normal levels explains the dramatically increased esophageal cancer risk in drinkers with ALDH2*2. (4) Pharmacological parallel — the drug disulfiram (Antabuse) used in alcohol aversion therapy works by the same principle: it irreversibly inhibits ALDH2, causing acetaldehyde accumulation and unpleasant symptoms upon alcohol consumption. (5) ADH1B*2 (a fast-acting variant common in East Asians) further worsens the effect by producing acetaldehyde more rapidly.
